explain.rule: Use 'going to' for plans and intentions already decided before speaking, and for predictions based on present evidence. Form: subject + am/is/are + going to + base verb.
explain.why_matters: Going to is essential for talking about your plans and things you can see are about to happen.
explain.hebrew_note: Hebrew has one future tense for all future meanings. English separates: will for spontaneous decisions, going to for pre-planned intentions and evidence-based predictions.
explain.tip: Already decided or can see the evidence right now? Use going to. Just your opinion or a spontaneous decision? Use will.

good_examples.sentence: I am going to visit my aunt next month — I already bought the ticket.
good_examples.audio_text: I am going to visit my aunt next month — I already bought the ticket.

good_examples.sentence: Watch out! That car is going to hit the wall!
good_examples.audio_text: Watch out! That car is going to hit the wall!

good_examples.sentence: Are you going to apply for that job?
good_examples.audio_text: Are you going to apply for that job?

good_examples.sentence: They are not going to finish on time — they are too far behind.
good_examples.audio_text: They are not going to finish on time — they are too far behind.

good_examples.sentence: She is going to have a baby in March.
good_examples.audio_text: She is going to have a baby in March.

good_examples.sentence: We are going to redecorate the flat this summer — we have already chosen the colours.
good_examples.audio_text: We are going to redecorate the flat this summer — we have already chosen the colours.

good_examples.sentence: Look at him — he is going to fall off that ladder!
good_examples.audio_text: Look at him — he is going to fall off that ladder!

good_examples.sentence: Is it going to be a long meeting? I need to be home by six.
good_examples.audio_text: Is it going to be a long meeting? I need to be home by six.

bad_examples.wrong: I am going to to visit Paris.
bad_examples.correct: I am going to visit Paris.
bad_examples.reason: Never 'going to to' — going to is followed directly by the base verb

bad_examples.wrong: She is going visit her parents.
bad_examples.correct: She is going to visit her parents.
bad_examples.reason: Don't forget 'to' — the full form is going to + base verb

bad_examples.correct: They are going to arrive soon.
bad_examples.reason: Don't forget am/is/are — going to needs the auxiliary verb
After going to, the verb must be base form

bad_examples.wrong: 'The phone is ringing.' 'I am going to answer it!'
bad_examples.correct: 'The phone is ringing.' 'I will answer it!'
bad_examples.reason: Spontaneous decision made at the moment → use will, not going to

exercises.question: I ___ (visit) my doctor next week — I already have an appointment.
exercises.answer: am going to visit
exercises.hint: Already planned → going to
exercises.explanation: Am + going to + visit. The appointment confirms a prior plan.
exercises.hebrew_note: כבר תכננת מראש — am going to visit

exercises.question: Look at those dark clouds! It ___ (rain).
exercises.answer: is going to rain
exercises.hint: Visible evidence → going to for prediction
exercises.explanation: Is + going to + rain. The evidence (dark clouds) is visible right now.

exercises.question: ___ (you / apply) for the new manager position?
exercises.answer: Are you going to apply
exercises.hint: Question about plans → Are + you + going to + base verb
exercises.explanation: Are + you + going to + apply.

exercises.question: She ___ (not / take) the job — the salary is too low.
exercises.answer: is not going to take
exercises.hint: Negative plan → is not going to + base verb
exercises.explanation: Is not going to + take. She has already decided.

exercises.question: They ___ (get) married in June — they've booked the venue.
exercises.answer: are going to get
exercises.hint: Already arranged → going to
exercises.explanation: Are + going to + get. Venue booked = prior arrangement.

exercises.question: Watch out — he ___ (drop) those glasses!
exercises.answer: is going to drop
exercises.hint: You can see it about to happen → going to
exercises.explanation: Is + going to + drop. Immediate prediction from visible evidence.

exercises.question: We ___ (not / be) in the office on Friday — we have a team event.
exercises.answer: are not going to be
exercises.hint: Planned absence → are not going to + base verb
exercises.explanation: Are not going to + be.

exercises.question: ___ (it / be) a long journey?
exercises.answer: Is it going to be
exercises.hint: Question about anticipated situation
exercises.explanation: Is + it + going to + be.

exercises.question: I ___ (start) learning Spanish — I enrolled in a course yesterday.
exercises.answer: am going to start
exercises.hint: Already enrolled = prior plan → going to
exercises.explanation: Am + going to + start.

exercises.question: The company ___ (launch) its new product in the spring.
exercises.answer: is going to launch
exercises.hint: Company = it, planned event → is going to
exercises.explanation: Is + going to + launch.

exercises.question: ___ (your parents / sell) their house and move to the coast?
exercises.answer: Are your parents going to sell
exercises.hint: Question about plan
exercises.explanation: Are + your parents + going to + sell.

exercises.question: He ___ (not / pass) the exam — he hasn't studied at all.
exercises.answer: is not going to pass
exercises.hint: Evidence-based negative prediction
exercises.explanation: Is not going to + pass. Evidence: no studying.

exercises.question: Look at the sky! Choose the best sentence:
exercises.answer: It is going to snow — the clouds are very low.
exercises.hint: Visible evidence → going to
exercises.explanation: Going to is for predictions with visible evidence. The clouds are the evidence.
distractors: It will snow — the clouds are very low.

exercises.question: 'I've just remembered — it's my mother's birthday tomorrow.' Choose the best response:
exercises.answer: Oh no! I'll buy her some flowers on the way home.
exercises.hint: Spontaneous decision made right now → which form?
exercises.explanation: Will — spontaneous decision just made. Going to would mean you had already planned to buy flowers.
distractors: Oh no! I am going to buy her some flowers.
Oh no! I buy her some flowers.
Oh no! I am buying her some flowers.

exercises.question: She ___ (not) the presentation — she looks terrible.
exercises.answer: is not going to give
exercises.hint: Evidence-based prediction
exercises.explanation: Is not going to + give. Her appearance is the evidence.

exercises.question: When ___ the new office?
exercises.answer: are they going to open
exercises.hint: Question about plan with are...going to
exercises.explanation: Are + they + going to + open. Planned future event.

exercises.question: Which sentence shows a plan made BEFORE speaking?
exercises.answer: We are going to spend two weeks in Japan — we bought the tickets in January.
exercises.hint: Evidence of prior decision?
exercises.explanation: Buying tickets in January proves the plan existed before speaking. Going to is correct.
distractors: I think it'll be cold tomorrow.
Will you help me with this?

exercises.question: Is the form 'gonna' correct in formal English?
exercises.answer: No — 'gonna' is informal spoken English only
exercises.hint: Register awareness
exercises.explanation: 'Gonna' is an informal spoken reduction of 'going to'. Never use it in formal writing or examinations.

exercises.question: Find and fix the mistake: 'She is going to to leave at five.'
exercises.answer: She is going to leave at five.
exercises.hint: Look at the double 'to'
exercises.explanation: Never 'going to to' — going to is followed directly by the base verb.

exercises.question: Find and fix the mistake: 'They going to arrive next week.'
exercises.answer: They are going to arrive next week.
exercises.hint: What is missing before 'going to'?
exercises.explanation: Are is missing. Need am/is/are before going to.

exercises.question: Find and fix: 'Are you going to comes to the party?'
exercises.answer: Are you going to come to the party?
exercises.hint: What form does the verb take after 'going to'?
exercises.explanation: Base verb after going to: come — not comes.

exercises.question: Find and fix: 'Look — he is going to fell!'
exercises.answer: Look — he is going to fall!
exercises.hint: After going to, use the base verb
exercises.explanation: Going to + base verb. Fall — not fell (past form).

exercises.question: Put the words in the correct order:
exercises.answer: I am going to retire next year.
exercises.hint: I + am going to + base verb + time
exercises.explanation: I + am going to + retire + next year.

exercises.question: Put the words in the correct order:
exercises.answer: She isn't going to pass the exam.
exercises.hint: Subject + isn't going to + base verb + object
exercises.explanation: She + isn't going to + pass + the exam.

exercises.question: Put the words in the correct order:
exercises.answer: When are you going to move?
exercises.hint: Wh-question: when + are + you + going to + base verb
exercises.explanation: When + are + you + going to + move?

exercises.question: Put the words in the correct order:
exercises.answer: Look, it is going to rain.
exercises.hint: Prediction with evidence: it + is going to + base verb
exercises.explanation: Look + it + is going to + rain.

exercises.question: Put the words in the correct order:
exercises.answer: They are going to open a new restaurant.
exercises.hint: Subject + are going to + base verb + object
exercises.explanation: They + are going to + open + a new restaurant.

speak.sentence: I am going to apply for a new job next month.
speak.phonetic_hints: 'Apply' = uh-PLY. Stress on second syllable. 'Month' = MUNTH.

speak.sentence: Watch out! That child is going to run into the road!
chunks: That child is going to run
speak.phonetic_hints: Watch — round your lips for W. 'Road' = ROHD. 'Child' = CHYLD — one syllable.

speak.sentence: Are you going to tell her what happened, or should I?
chunks: Are you going to tell her
speak.phonetic_hints: 'Happened' = HAP-und. 'Should' = SHUD — the L is silent. Stress: ARE you going to.

speak.sentence: We are going to spend our holiday in Portugal this year.
chunks: We are going to spend our holiday

speak.sentence: She isn't going to accept that offer — the conditions are terrible.
chunks: She isn't going to accept that offer
speak.phonetic_hints: 'Accept' = ak-SEPT. Stress second syllable. 'Conditions' = kun-DISH-unz. Stress second syllable.

speak.sentence: Look at those clouds — it is definitely going to snow tonight.
chunks: it is definitely going to snow tonight.

speak.sentence: When are you going to make a decision about the house?
chunks: When are you going to make a decision

speak.sentence: They are going to completely redesign their website next quarter.
chunks: They are going to completely redesign
